# Containers from first principles 🟡

**Goal.** Understand what a container *is* by assembling one primitive at a time, then meet the managers that package those primitives for you.

**Audience:** intermediate; comfortable reading a little C  
**Effort:** ⏱ half-day+  
**Prerequisites:** [Shell fluency — from lost at the prompt to sculpting text](path-shell-fluency.md)

| # | Lab | You'll learn | Checkpoint (observable) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | [`chroot-examples/chroot-debian-bookworm.toml`](../chroot-examples/chroot-debian-bookworm.toml) 🔑 | a chroot swaps the filesystem root — and *only* that; the process keeps every other namespace. | `enter` the chroot: `cat /etc/os-release` says Debian, but `ps -e` still lists the host's PIDs — so it is not isolation. |
| 2 | [`chroot-examples/chroot-host-copy-busybox.toml`](../chroot-examples/chroot-host-copy-busybox.toml) 🔑 | a root filesystem is just a directory of files — no debootstrap required. | the tree holds only BusyBox + a few `/etc` files; name which came from the host copy vs were fabricated. |
| 3 | [`chroot-breakout`](../chroot-breakout/) | a chroot is not a security boundary; `pivot_root` + mount/UTS namespaces are. | `breakout` prints the escape banner, then `ls /` shows the FULL container root — versus the sparse jail it showed a moment earlier.<br>🔎 auto-checkable → marker `Time to break things` |
| 4 | [`exploring-containers/part-2-namespaces`](../exploring-containers/part-2-namespaces/) | namespaces scope what a process can SEE — IPC and network, by hand. | a fresh `unshare --ipc ./processB` reads an EMPTY segment while the host namespace still sees the message; two net namespaces ping across a veth at 0% loss. |
| 5 | [`exploring-containers/part-3-pid-cgroups-users`](../exploring-containers/part-3-pid-cgroups-users/) | PID/cgroup/user namespaces + a memory cgroup finish the container; the user namespace is the keystone that lets an unprivileged user make the rest. | inside `unshare --pid --fork --mount-proc` your shell is PID 1; `podman run --memory=50m` + `alloc.c` is OOM-killed (exit 137); `unshare --user --map-root-user` shows uid 0 mapped.<br>🔎 auto-checkable → marker `EXIT=137` 🖥️ |
| 6 | [`chroot-examples/chroot-nspawn-managed.toml`](../chroot-examples/chroot-nspawn-managed.toml) 🔑 | systemd-nspawn/machinectl assemble those primitives into a managed, bootable machine. | `machinectl list` shows the machine; `systemd-nspawn -b` boots it to a login prompt. |
| 7 | [`podman-examples/podman-pod-3svc.toml`](../podman-examples/podman-pod-3svc.toml) 🪶 | a real rootless engine builds that whole namespace stack for you. | three containers in one pod reach each other over `localhost`; map each shared namespace back to one you built by hand. |
| 8 | [`lxd-examples/lxd-plain-single.toml`](../lxd-examples/lxd-plain-single.toml) | the system-container manager — a persistent machine, contrasted with the app-container pod. | `incus launch` the Alpine container; `incus list` shows it RUNNING; contrast its lifecycle with the podman pod. |

### Optional side-quests

- [`linux-proc-vfs-internals`](../linux-proc-vfs-internals/) — go deeper on what `/proc` actually is, in C + strace — its cgroup-v2 OOM demo mirrors step 5.
